title: Listening - Part 1
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.
instructions: You will hear three different extracts. For questions 1 – 6, choose the answer (A, B or C) which fits best according to what you hear. There are two questions for each extract.

extracts.context: You hear two coworkers, a man and a woman, discussing a new office policy on 'meeting-free Wednesdays'.
extracts.audio_script: Man: So, the first 'quiet Wednesday' is tomorrow. I’m actually looking forward to getting some real work done. Woman: I’m skeptical, Thomas. [1]I have a feeling that people will just cram twice as many meetings into Tuesday and Thursday instead[/1]. Man: You’re such a pessimist! I think it’ll encourage people to be more mindful of whether a meeting is actually necessary. Woman: Or it’ll just lead to an explosion of urgent 'instant messages' that are just as disruptive. Man: Well, [2]the management's intention is to provide 'deep work' time[/2], and I for one am going to guard it fiercely. Woman: Good luck with that. I give it three weeks before a 'critical' project update breaks the rule. Man: We’ll see. If it works, it might even become two days a week.

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: So, the first 'quiet Wednesday' is tomorrow. I’m actually looking forward to getting some real work done.

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: I’m skeptical, Thomas. I have a feeling that people will just cram twice as many meetings into Tuesday and Thursday instead.

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: You’re such a pessimist! I think it’ll encourage people to be more mindful of whether a meeting is actually necessary.

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: Or it’ll just lead to an explosion of urgent 'instant messages' that are just as disruptive.

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: Well, the management's intention is to provide 'deep work' time, and I for one am going to guard it fiercely.

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: Good luck with that. I give it three weeks before a 'critical' project update breaks the rule.

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: We’ll see. If it works, it might even become two days a week.

questions.question: The woman suggests that the new policy might lead to...

options.A: a decrease in overall employee productivity.
options.B: an uneven distribution of the weekly workload.
options.C: a total breakdown in team communication.

questions.explanation: She predicts that meetings will be 'crammed' into the other days, implying an uneven workload distribution across the week.

questions.question: What is the stated goal of the management with this policy?

options.A: To reduce the company's electricity consumption.
options.B: To improve the mental health of the staff.
options.C: To facilitate periods of uninterrupted focus.

questions.explanation: The man mentions 'deep work' time, which is a common C1 term for high-focus, uninterrupted work.

extracts.context: You hear two university students, a man and a woman, discussing their shared accommodation.
extracts.audio_script: Man: Look, Olivia, we need to talk about the kitchen. It’s becoming a bit of a disaster zone. Woman: I know, I know. But [3]with the finals coming up, I’ve just been grabbing a sandwich and leaving my plates until the morning[/3]. Man: It’s not just the plates, though. It’s the recycling piling up and the fridge that hasn't been cleaned in months. Woman: [4]I’m happy to do a deep clean once the exams are over, but right now I can barely find time to sleep[/4]. Man: I get that we’re all stressed, but a messy environment just adds to the tension. Maybe we could hire a cleaner for a one-off visit? Woman: On a student budget? You must be joking. Let's just agree on a 10-minute 'power-clean' every evening. Man: I suppose that’s a fair compromise.

dialogue.speaker: woman

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: I suppose that’s a fair compromise.

questions.question: The woman justifies her recent lack of tidiness by citing...

options.A: a temporary change in her dietary habits.
options.C: a misunderstanding of the cleaning schedule.

questions.explanation: She mentions 'finals coming up' (exams), which explains why she has been neglecting the chores.

questions.question: What is the woman's attitude towards the man's suggestion of a professional cleaner?

options.A: She considers it an unnecessary expense.
options.B: She believes it wouldn't solve the underlying problem.
options.C: She thinks it's a practical solution for the exam period.

questions.explanation: She dismisses it with 'On a student budget? You must be joking', indicating she finds it too expensive.

extracts.context: You hear two friends discussing a new documentary about the history of the internet.
extracts.audio_script: Man: That documentary last night was a bit of a wake-up call, wasn't it? Woman: It was. [5]I never realized quite how much the early pioneers were motivated by pure idealism[/5], rather than profit. Man: I know, it’s a far cry from the corporate-dominated landscape we have today. Woman: [6]I found the archive footage of those early chat rooms particularly poignant[/6]. Everyone was so polite! Man: Well, that’s because there were only about fifty people on the whole network back then. Woman: True. It’s just sad to see how the 'global village' dream has kind of turned into a series of echo chambers. Man: I think the documentary was a bit too nostalgic, though. It ignored all the ways the internet has made life easier for the average person. Woman: Maybe, but it’s good to be reminded of what we’ve lost along the way.

questions.question: What surprised the woman about the creators of the early internet?
questions.explanation: She mentions being surprised that they were 'motivated by pure idealism rather than profit'.

questions.question: What did the woman find especially moving in the documentary?

options.B: The predictions for the future of the web.
options.C: The historical recordings of online interactions.

questions.explanation: She calls the 'archive footage of those early chat rooms' 'particularly poignant' (moving).
